does: This paper introduces MANTA, a multi-turn adversarial benchmark containing 1,088 five-turn conversations designed to evaluate how large language models reason about animal welfare across sustained adversarial pressure. The benchmark progresses from implicit welfare scenarios to explicit prompts followed by three rounds of adversarial challenges drawn from five categories: Social, Cultural, Economic, Pragmatic, and Epistemic. Models are scored on two dimensions: Animal Welfare Value Stability (AWVS), measuring whether models maintain animal welfare commitments under pressure, and Animal Welfare Moral Sensitivity (AWMS), measuring whether models spontaneously recognize welfare considerations in everyday contexts. The authors evaluate seven frontier models to assess both explicit value alignment and robustness to adversarial manipulation.
connects: This work extends prior evaluation frameworks like AnimalHarmBench that assess harmful content avoidance through single-turn explicit questions, addressing documented limitations in how current benchmarks measure AI alignment and values. It builds on research examining alignment degradation under adversarial pressure and connects to broader work on evaluating whether language models genuinely hold moral commitments versus simply pattern-matching safe responses. The multi-turn adversarial structure draws from adversarial robustness testing in machine learning while applying it to the domain of value alignment and ethical reasoning. This research also engages with growing literature on moral sensitivity in AI systems and how models handle value conflicts when economic, cultural, or pragmatic considerations compete with stated ethical commitments.
enables: This benchmark enables more rigorous evaluation of value alignment in deployed AI systems by capturing failure modes that single-turn evaluations miss, particularly degradation under sustained pressure. Future work can use MANTA to develop more robust instruction-tuning methods and fine-tuning techniques that maintain moral commitments across adversarial contexts rather than just surface-level compliance. The framework points toward evaluating genuine moral reasoning versus superficial alignment, potentially informing better training procedures for values that withstand real-world deployment scenarios. Additionally, the taxonomy of adversarial pressure types provides a foundation for studying how different categories of pressure affect various ethical domains beyond animal welfare, enabling broader research on which values remain stable across different conflict scenarios.
